metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in S3 that `adapters/__init__.py` must be *updated* to import each shipped adapter (creating the auto-import mechanism, not relying on one that already exists), and add this file to S3's explicit edit-set.
evidence: S3 story description: 'Registration happens at import of adapters/jira/backend.py; select_backend imports that module (via the existing adapters/__init__ that imports each shipped adapter) before resolving, so the registry is populated deterministically regardless of import order.'
Actual file src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/adapters/__init__.py contains only a comment block — no import of any backend module, no auto-discovery mechanism.
If this auto-import is not created in S3, select_backend() will call into an empty registry and raise, because JiraBackend registration only fires on import of adapters/jira/backend.py.
findings.finding: S3 describes the registry population mechanism as relying on 'the existing `adapters/__init__` that imports each shipped adapter,' but the actual `adapters/__init__.py` contains only a comment with no such auto-import. If an implementer reads 'existing' and does not add the auto-import trigger in S3, the registry will be empty at runtime and `select_backend()` will raise a key-not-found error even when `config.reconciler.backend == 'jira'` — breaking the default flow with no compile-time signal.
findings.location: S3 (ticket 94f9-a9a0-7475-4e75) Approach section: 'select_backend imports that module (via the existing adapters/__init__ that imports each shipped adapter)'
scenarios: Implementer reads 'existing adapters/__init__' and, finding it contains only comments, either skips adding the auto-import (assuming it exists elsewhere) or is confused about where to add it — registry stays empty.
Integration test calling select_backend(load_config()) on the default config raises a registry-key-not-found error, despite the config being valid and the JiraBackend class having been defined in S2.
findings.suggested_fix: In S3's Approach and Scope sections, replace 'via the existing `adapters/__init__` that imports each shipped adapter' with 'by updating `adapters/__init__.py` to import each shipped adapter sub-package (e.g. `from . import jira`) — this auto-import is added in S3 and is the mechanism that triggers registration.' Add `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/adapters/__init__.py` to S3's file_impact list.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Update parent AC-4 to replace '~29 test patch strings' with S5's authoritative taxonomy (zero mock.patch string patches; ~18 path-constant files + ~18 dotted-import files in categories 2+3, deduped union migrated atomically).
evidence: Parent AC-4: 'all ~29 test patch strings are migrated with no re-export shims (S5).'

S5 Context: 'the first draft's ~29 mock.patch strings was wrong; there are zero mock.patch("rebar_reconciler.acli…") string patches. Three distinct categories: 1. ~23 files use patch.object ... path-agnostic; 2. ~18 files hardcode importlib.util.spec_from_file_location; 3. ~18 files use direct dotted imports.'
S5 replaces the parent's metric ('~29 patch strings') with a different metric (deduped union of categories 2+3) without updating the parent AC.
container pairing: parent + 6673-7636-a116-4f90/94f9-a9a0-7475-4e75/ce4d-c3ca-5412-4798/dfb9-d2d1-455d-48d9/ef33-eaf6-a545-4bfb
findings.finding: Parent AC-4 states 'all ~29 test patch strings are migrated with no re-export shims (S5)' but S5 (dfb9) explicitly contradicts this, saying 'the first draft's ~29 mock.patch strings was wrong; there are zero mock.patch string patches' — replacing the concept with categories 2 (~18 path-constant files) and 3 (~18 dotted-import files). The parent AC uses erroneous terminology that S5 does not reconcile upward.
findings.location: child dfb9-d2d1-455d-48d9 — Context/Problem ('characterized correctly — the first draft's ~29 mock.patch strings was wrong'); parent Acceptance Criteria AC-4
scenarios: A completion reviewer reading the parent AC against S5's delivery would find a metric mismatch: the parent says 29 patch strings migrated; S5 says zero patch strings exist (different category entirely). The completion check would be confused about what to verify.
If S5 authors interpret 'patch strings' as mock.patch string targets and grep for them, they find zero — technically satisfying S5's claim but not the parent AC's intent.
findings.suggested_fix: In parent AC-4, replace 'all ~29 test patch strings are migrated with no re-export shims' with 'the category-2 (~18 path-constant) and category-3 (~18 dotted-import) test references are rewritten to adapters.jira.* paths (zero mock.patch string patches exist; the ~23 patch.object _load_acli tests are path-agnostic and verified still-green), with no re-export shims — per S5's corrected taxonomy.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either update parent AC-7 to acknowledge the _config_schema.py _as_choice edit as a required step for each new backend, or redesign S3 so the allowed-choice set is derived from the registry at config-load time (eliminating the core edit requirement).
evidence: Parent AC-7: 'adding a backend requires only a new adapters/<x>/ implementing the pinned interface.'
S3 Approach: 'A second backend adds its key to both the _as_choice set and the registry.' The _as_choice set is in src/rebar/_config_schema.py, a core file outside adapters/.
S3 Scope: 'The static choice-set ({"jira"} today) is the config-load guard; the registry below does the authoritative runtime object lookup.'
S3's _as_choice constraint requires editing src/rebar/_config_schema.py for every new backend, which is a core edit, not an adapters/<x>/ edit.
container pairing: parent + 6673-7636-a116-4f90/94f9-a9a0-7475-4e75/ce4d-c3ca-5412-4798/dfb9-d2d1-455d-48d9/ef33-eaf6-a545-4bfb
findings.finding: Parent AC-7 states 'adding a backend requires only a new adapters/<x>/ implementing the pinned interface — verified by the proof-of-seam FakeBackend passing the contract suite with no core edit,' but S3 (94f9) design requires editing _config_schema.py (a core app-level config file, not adapters/<x>/) to add the new key to the _as_choice static set for every new backend.
findings.location: child 94f9-a9a0-7475-4e75 — Approach ('A second backend adds its key to both the _as_choice set and the registry'); parent Acceptance Criteria AC-7
scenarios: Epic be74 author implementing a GitHub backend must edit src/rebar/_config_schema.py to add 'github' to the _as_choice set — a core file change that the parent AC says should not be required.

The FakeBackend contract-suite proof (ce4d) does not exercise the _as_choice path at all (tests bypass config-load), so it does not actually verify 'no core edit' for a real new backend — the proof-of-seam is incomplete relative to AC-7's claim.
findings.suggested_fix: Option A (update AC-7): Revise parent AC-7 to read 'adding a backend requires implementing adapters/<x>/ and adding its key to the _as_choice set in _config_schema.py and the registry.' Option B (redesign S3): derive the _as_choice allowed-set dynamically from the populated registry at config-load time rather than hardcoding it, so only the adapters/<x>/ addition is needed.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Remove the two stale file_impact entries in S3 (94f9-a9a0-7475-4e75) — `rebar_reconciler/config.py` and `rebar_reconciler/_config_schema.py` — and replace them with the correct target `src/rebar/_config_schema.py` (the app-level config schema where ReconcilerConfig lives, as the description body already states).
evidence: S3 file_impact: {path: 'src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/config.py', reason: 'Add reconciler.backend key, default jira'}
S3 file_impact: {path: 'src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/_config_schema.py', reason: 'Static-frozenset validation of backend key (stdlib-only schema)'}

S3 description body: 'No src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/config.py change and no new rebar_reconciler/_config_schema.py — the earlier draft named both in error; the real config home is the app-level src/rebar/_config_schema.py above.'
findings.finding: S3's file_impact list includes 'src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/config.py' (reason: 'Add reconciler.backend key, default jira') and 'src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/_config_schema.py' (reason: 'Static-frozenset validation of backend key') — but the S3 description body explicitly states: 'No src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/config.py change and no new rebar_reconciler/_config_schema.py — the earlier draft named both in error.' The contradictory file_impact entries will cause an implementer following the file list to open and edit the wrong files.
findings.location: S3 (94f9-a9a0-7475-4e75) file_impact list vs. description body
scenarios: An implementer or reviewer scanning the file_impact list (the standard harness input) edits rebar_reconciler/config.py instead of the app-level src/rebar/_config_schema.py — the actual target — producing a change that passes lint but fails at runtime because the wrong config layer is modified
findings.suggested_fix: Replace S3 file_impact entries: remove `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/config.py` and `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/_config_schema.py`; add `src/rebar/_config_schema.py` with reason 'Add backend field to ReconcilerConfig + _as_choice coercer in reconciler coercer table'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly specify in S3 which file gets the 'import adapters/jira/backend' side-effect import (most likely adapters/__init__.py or _backend_registry.py itself), and add it to S3's file_impact and scope so the registry-population path is unambiguously owned.
evidence: S3 Approach: 'select_backend imports that module (via the existing adapters/__init__ that imports each shipped adapter) before resolving, so the registry is populated deterministically regardless of import order'
Actual adapters/__init__.py content: only a comment block, no imports of any adapter submodule
No story in S1-S5 names 'add adapter import to adapters/__init__.py' as an explicit task
findings.finding: S3 states that select_backend() populates the registry deterministically because 'Registration happens at import of adapters/jira/backend.py; select_backend imports that module (via the existing adapters/__init__ that imports each shipped adapter).' However, the actual adapters/__init__.py in the repo contains only a comment and does NOT import adapters/jira/backend.py or any adapter submodule. The plan's stated mechanism for ensuring registry population — 'the existing adapters/__init__ that imports each shipped adapter' — does not exist. No plan artifact specifies who adds that import to adapters/__init__, when, or as part of which story.
findings.location: S3 (94f9-a9a0-7475-4e75) Approach section; src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/adapters/__init__.py
scenarios: S3 is implemented; select_backend() is called; adapters/__init__ does not import adapters/jira/backend.py; registry is empty; select_backend raises a registry-not-found error instead of returning JiraBackend — the default case breaks silently
findings.suggested_fix: In S3 scope and file_impact, add: 'Update src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/adapters/__init__.py to import adapters.jira.backend (triggering JiraBackend registration at package import time).' Alternatively, have _backend_registry.py's select_backend() explicitly import the jira adapter module when the requested key is 'jira', rather than relying on a side-effect import chain.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the capability opt-in dispatch mechanism in ADR 0035 §(d) or the S2 story: state explicitly whether `isinstance(backend, SupportsLinks)`, a `backend.capabilities: frozenset[str]` property, a sentinel-method pattern, or another mechanism is used — and reconcile that choice with S2's 'not via structural introspection' constraint.
evidence: Epic plan: 'opt-in capability Protocols' listed as a decided design element with no dispatch spec
S2 (ce4d) Testing: 'a backend that does not implement SupportsLinks is never asked to sync links — observed via behavior, not via structural introspection'
S4 (6673) routes consumers through the Backend port but does not mention capability-conditional routing
No existing codebase precedent for behavioral (non-introspective) capability dispatch found via search
Python typing.Protocol structural subtyping requires isinstance() or hasattr() for runtime checks — contradicting the 'not structural introspection' constraint
findings.finding: The epic asserts opt-in capability Protocols (`SupportsLinks`, `SupportsComments`, `SupportsIncremental`) are dispatched 'observed via behavior — a backend that does not implement SupportsLinks is never asked to sync links', but never specifies the dispatch mechanism. Python structural Protocols alone do not provide runtime dispatch without `isinstance`/`hasattr`, yet S2's contract suite explicitly rejects 'structural introspection'. No story in S1–S5 owns the routing logic that consults capability at call time, leaving an unverified assertion about how the core actually skips unsupported operations.
findings.location: Design section ('opt-in capability Protocols'); S2 ticket ce4d Testing section ('observed via behavior … not via structural introspection')
scenarios: S4 implementor must decide how the core knows a backend doesn't support links — if they use isinstance(backend, SupportsLinks), that is structural introspection (contradicting S2's contract spec); if they use a separate flag or sentinel, that is an undocumented design choice not in scope for any story.

The FakeBackend contract test for capability dispatch (S2 AC: 'a backend that does not implement SupportsLinks is never asked to sync links') cannot be written until the dispatch mechanism is specified, making S2's contract suite incomplete without a new design decision.
findings.suggested_fix: The simplest grounded approach: add a `capabilities() -> frozenset[str]` method to the `Backend` facade (returning e.g. `{"links", "comments", "incremental"}`), which all implementations return explicitly. This is behavioral (not structural introspection), easy to test, easy for `FakeBackend` to override, and does not require `isinstance` anywhere in the core. Record this in ADR 0035 §(d).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the capability-detection protocol in the Backend facade definition (epic Design section or ADR §(d) §capability-detection): name whether core uses isinstance(backend, SupportsX), a Backend.capabilities() -> frozenset method, or another mechanism — and co-locate that rule with the Backend Protocol spec.
evidence: S2 Testing: 'a capability Protocol's methods are exercised only when the backend advertises it (observed via behavior — a backend that does not implement SupportsLinks is never asked to sync links), not via structural introspection'
Epic plan Design section names SupportsLinks/SupportsComments/SupportsIncremental as 'opt-in capability Protocols' but specifies no detection rule
ADR §(d) does not mention capability detection at all
findings.finding: The capability-detection protocol — how the core determines at runtime whether a given Backend advertises an opt-in capability (SupportsLinks, SupportsComments, SupportsIncremental) — is described only in S2 Testing as a behavioral outcome ('a backend that does not implement SupportsLinks is never asked to sync links — observed via behavior, not via structural introspection') but the decision rule itself (isinstance check? hasattr? explicit capabilities() method on Backend?) is never co-located with the Backend facade spec in the epic plan or in ADR §(d). This is a processing protocol not co-located with the schema that needs it.
findings.location: S2 (ce4d-c3ca-5412-4798) Testing section; Epic plan 'Design (decided)' section
scenarios: S4 implementer routing outbound_links.py through the Backend port needs to know whether to call isinstance(backend, SupportsLinks) or check a capabilities() set — the plan gives contradictory signals ('not via structural introspection' but also no alternative mechanism is named)

evidence: 1 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).
findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a method-signature table for all five role Protocols and three capability Protocols to ADR 0035 §(d) (or to the epic plan's Design section) — at minimum: method name, parameter names and types, return type, and any raises contract — so S2 has a normative target rather than re-deriving the surface from grep.
evidence: ADR §(d) as read from repo contains only 5 prose bullet points ('a transport/CRUD module...', 'outbound field mapping...', etc.) with no Protocol method signatures
Epic plan states 'Five role Protocols (TicketTransport, OutboundMapper, InboundMapper, FieldSanitizer, IdentityConvention) behind one Backend facade' but enumerates no method signatures

S2 description says 'Derive each Protocol's method signatures from the de-facto surface the core already calls' — deferring the normative signature vocabulary entirely to implementation-time derivation
S1 AC says '§(d) names all five role Protocols, the Backend facade, RemoteRef, and the registry' but does not require method signatures to be pinned in the ADR
findings.finding: The five role Protocols (TicketTransport, OutboundMapper, InboundMapper, FieldSanitizer, IdentityConvention) and three capability Protocols (SupportsLinks, SupportsComments, SupportsIncremental) are referenced by name throughout the epic plan but their method-level signatures (method names, parameter types, return types) are never enumerated in any plan artifact. ADR 0035 §(d) as it currently exists in the repo is a prose sketch with five prose bullets and no method signatures; S1 is the story that will pin them, but the epic plan itself — which is what S2 implementers will read — provides no signature vocabulary. An S2 implementer who derives signatures independently from 'the de-facto surface the core already calls' may produce a different interface than the S1 author intended, with no cross-check surface.
findings.location: Epic plan 'Design (decided)' section; ADR 0035 §(d) (docs/adr/0035-reconciler-vendor-adapter-seam.md lines 119-131)
scenarios: S2 implementer derives TicketTransport.create() with one signature; a future S4 reviewer finds a mismatch with what S1 wrote into the ADR; no plan-level artifact resolves the conflict
Epic be74 (second backend) author implements SupportsIncremental with wrong method name because the signed interface was never enumerated at epic level
findings.suggested_fix: In the epic plan's 'Design (decided)' section, add a collapsed table: | Protocol | Method | Signature | Notes | — populated from the S2 description's 'derive from de-facto surface' guidance, then reference that table as the normative spec in S1's AC.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly identify and update the user-facing configuration documentation (config schema, README config section, or operator guide) as part of S3's definition of done.
evidence: 'add `reconciler.backend` config (static-key validated) + an in-tree registry + `select_backend()` + `JiraBackend` registration'
The AC notes the default reproduces today's behavior, but no doc artifact (config reference, schema annotation, README section) is called out.
ABSENCE: no 'update config docs / schema' step in S3 or anywhere in the epic.
findings.finding: The new `config.reconciler.backend` configuration key introduced in S3 is a user-facing config change but the plan names no target location for user-facing documentation (config schema file, README, or operator guide).
findings.location: S3 description: 'add `reconciler.backend` config (static-key validated)'
scenarios: An operator upgrading rebar sees a new config key in validation errors but has no reference doc explaining valid values, the default, or the intended future extension points.
The config schema file (if one exists) becomes stale, causing schema-validation failures or misleading IDE hints.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a step in S5 (or S1 when updating ADR 0035 §(d)) to grep/audit docs, ADR prose, and inline comments for old module paths (acli, adf, outbound_fields, comment_limits at root) and update any references found.
evidence: 'physically relocate `acli*`/`adf`/`outbound_fields`/`comment_limits` into `adapters/jira/`'
ADR 0035 and Phase 1 documentation (44be) may cite these modules by their original package-root paths.
ABSENCE: the plan lists code-level repointing (inbound_fields.py, outbound_comments.py) and test patch strings, but no mention of auditing doc/comment/ADR references to old paths.
findings.finding: S5 renames/relocates four modules (acli*, adf, outbound_fields, comment_limits) but the plan does not include a step to audit and update any existing documentation or ADR prose that references those modules by their old (root-level) paths, risking stale references.
findings.location: S5 story and AC bullet referencing module relocations
scenarios: A developer reads ADR 0035 or a docstring that cites 'rebar_reconciler/acli.py' and follows a stale path, wasting time or drawing wrong architectural conclusions.
Automated doc-link checkers or grep-based audits surface false positives from un-updated prose.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate the opt-in capability Protocols (or state explicitly 'none in scope for Phase 2') so S2 has a complete interface target.
evidence: 'Five role Protocols (TicketTransport, OutboundMapper, InboundMapper, FieldSanitizer, IdentityConvention) behind one Backend facade, + opt-in capability Protocols.'
No subsequent section names or enumerates the opt-in protocols.
S2 AC mentions only the five named Protocols and contract suite — opt-in protocols absent.
findings.finding: The plan mentions 'opt-in capability Protocols' alongside the five named role Protocols but never enumerates them, names examples, or states the default (zero opt-in protocols). An executor implementing S2 cannot know whether to design any, or how many slots to leave.
findings.location: Design (decided) — 'Five role Protocols … + opt-in capability Protocols.'
scenarios: S2 executor designs only the five named protocols and ships; a later story assumes certain opt-in capabilities exist and must reopen S2.
Executor invents opt-in protocols not intended by the author, causing interface bloat before the first real second backend lands.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a bullet such as: 'Opt-in capability Protocols in scope for Phase 2: none — the extension point is reserved but no concrete capability protocols are defined here; they are deferred to epic be74.' Or list any that are needed (e.g. `BulkFetch`, `AttachmentUpload`) with their signatures.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the neutrality gate mechanism (e.g. 'an import-linter forbidden-modules rule', 'a pytest import-scan fixture', 'a ruff banned-imports config') so S4 has an unambiguous implementation target.
evidence: 'enforced by a neutrality gate on the **12 rewired core sites**'
No section names the tool, file, or rule that constitutes the gate.
'.github/module-size-limit.txt' is named for the LOC cap by analogy, but no equivalent artifact is named for the neutrality gate.
findings.finding: The AC requires the neutrality gate to enforce that core modules no longer import vendor modules, but the plan never specifies the gate's mechanism (import-linter rule, custom pytest check, ruff/flake8 plugin, CI script). An executor must choose without guidance.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — S4 bullet: 'enforced by a neutrality gate on the 12 rewired core sites'
scenarios: Executor uses a fragile grep in CI that breaks on refactors; a later PR accidentally reintroduces a vendor import undetected.
Executor picks import-linter and adds a .importlinter config; reviewer expected a pytest conftest check — the gate is re-litigated at review time.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the S4 description or AC: 'Neutrality gate: add a `[forbidden-modules]` contract in `.importlinter` (or equivalent) covering the 12 core site modules, run as part of `make lint`, ensuring no `adapters.jira.*` or root vendor-module import survives in those files.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `FakeBackend` (test-only backend implementing the pinned Protocols) explicitly to the S2 decomposition description so the deliverable appears in both the body and the AC.
evidence: S2 decomp: 'a thin JiraBackend (zero behavior change). No core rewiring.'
AC bullet 1: 'a contract suite passing against both JiraBackend and a test-only FakeBackend exist (S2)'
findings.finding: The S2 decomposition body describes 'a thin JiraBackend (zero behavior change)' but does not mention FakeBackend; however, the S2 AC bullet requires 'a contract suite passing against both JiraBackend and a test-only FakeBackend exist (S2).' FakeBackend is a real S2 deliverable that is absent from S2's description, making the decomposition body incomplete relative to its own AC.
findings.location: Child decomposition §S2 description vs. AC bullet 1
scenarios: Implementer reads S2 description and scopes only JiraBackend; FakeBackend surfaces as a surprise requirement at review time.
findings.suggested_fix: In S2's decomposition bullet, append: '…plus a test-only `FakeBackend` stub that implements the same Protocols, used by the contract suite.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add the `inbound_fields.py` and `outbound_comments.py` sibling-reference repointing explicitly to S5's decomposition description so it matches the S5 AC.
evidence: S5 decomp: 'physically relocate acli*/adf/outbound_fields/comment_limits into adapters/jira/, teach the file-location loader the new dir, and migrate the ~29 test patch strings atomically (no shims).'
AC bullet 4: 'the two non-moved root modules that reference them by sibling filename (inbound_fields.py, outbound_comments.py) are repointed'
findings.finding: The S5 AC bullet requires that 'the two non-moved root modules that reference them by sibling filename (inbound_fields.py, outbound_comments.py) are repointed,' but this repointing work is not mentioned anywhere in S5's decomposition description. It is an uncovered described deliverable gap in the decomposition body.
findings.location: Child decomposition §S5 description vs. AC bullet 4 (S5)
scenarios: Implementer completes S5 per the description, passes the file-move and patch-string migration, but misses repointing inbound_fields.py and outbound_comments.py, causing import failures caught only at suite run.
findings.suggested_fix: Append to S5 decomp: '…and repoint the two non-moved root modules (`inbound_fields.py`, `outbound_comments.py`) that import moved siblings by relative filename.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either add a criterion that verifies the opt-in capability Protocol(s) are defined (even if empty stubs) in S2, or explicitly descope them from this epic and note that they are deferred.
evidence: Design: 'Five role Protocols (TicketTransport, OutboundMapper, InboundMapper, FieldSanitizer, IdentityConvention) behind one Backend facade, + opt-in capability Protocols.'
AC bullet 1 (S2): 'the Backend Protocols, RemoteRef, and a contract suite passing against both JiraBackend and a test-only FakeBackend exist' — no mention of opt-in capability Protocols.
findings.finding: The Design section states 'Five role Protocols … + opt-in capability Protocols' as part of the Backend seam, but no AC bullet verifies the existence, shape, or usage of the opt-in capability Protocols. This is described work with no covering criterion.
findings.location: Design section ('Five role Protocols … + opt-in capability Protocols') vs. AC (all bullets)
scenarios: The opt-in capability Protocols are designed, then omitted from S2 implementation with no AC to catch the omission; the seam is incomplete for capability-optional backends (e.g. a read-only backend).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC sub-condition (under S4) that explicitly verifies no `"jira"` string literal remains in the backend-neutral core (or that all vendor discrimination goes through `RemoteRef`), covering the removal half of the RemoteRef migration.
evidence: Design: 'One new type RemoteRef{vendor, instance, remote_id} replaces the hardcoded "jira" literal.'

AC bullet 3: 'the backend-neutral core no longer imports adapters.jira.* or the root vendor modules — enforced by a neutrality gate on the 12 rewired core sites' — no mention of hardcoded literal removal.
findings.finding: The Design section states that RemoteRef 'replaces the hardcoded "jira" literal,' making this a migration (removal + replacement). No AC bullet explicitly verifies the *removal* of the hardcoded literal — the S4 AC checks core neutrality (no adapters.jira.* imports) but does not confirm the literal is gone. This violates the E1 migration criterion requiring criteria to verify both removal and replacement.
findings.location: Design section ('One new type RemoteRef{vendor, instance, remote_id} replaces the hardcoded "jira" literal') vs. AC bullet 3 (S4 neutrality gate)
scenarios: Core is routed through Backend but still sprinkles `== "jira"` literal checks; the seam is porous for a second backend even though all AC bullets pass.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the form of the 'neutrality gate' in the S4 decomposition description (e.g. 'a `tests/architecture/test_core_neutrality.py` using `importlib` or `ast` to assert no vendor imports in the 12 core modules, run as part of `make test`').
evidence: AC bullet 3: 'enforced by a neutrality gate on the 12 rewired core sites'

S4 decomp: 'route every core consumer through the Backend port: the 8 confirmed vendor-importing core modules and the 4 transport/construction sites' — no mention of how neutrality is enforced/verified.
findings.finding: The AC bullet for S4 references 'a neutrality gate on the 12 rewired core sites' as the enforcement mechanism, but neither S4's decomposition description nor any other section defines what form this gate takes (e.g. import-checker script, CI lint step, test assertion, grep in Makefile). The AC measures something whose artifact is unspecified, creating a cross-section ambiguity between the S4 description and the AC.
findings.location: AC bullet 3 (S4 neutrality gate) vs. Child decomposition §S4 description
scenarios: Two implementers independently pick different gate mechanisms (one writes a pytest import test, another adds a grep to Makefile); the gate form is unreviewed and may not be durable across refactors.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit back-out plan for S5: either (a) describe the revert-commit procedure and the conditions under which it is safe, or (b) adopt a two-phase approach (add shims in S5a, delete originals in S5b after one green CI cycle) so S5 is reversible at each sub-step.
evidence: 'physically relocate `acli*`/`adf`/`outbound_fields`/`comment_limits` into `adapters/jira/`'
'migrate the ~29 test patch strings atomically (no shims)'
The plan sequences S4 before S5 (expand-contract for callers), but once S5 lands and the originals are deleted without shims, the step is irreversible — no back-out mechanism is stated.
ABSENCE: no 'to revert S5, …' instruction, no temporary shim-then-delete two-step, no flag to re-enable old paths.
findings.finding: S5 performs a destructive, irreversible file relocation (acli*/adf/outbound_fields/comment_limits moved to adapters/jira/ with no re-export shims) but the plan provides no explicit back-out plan or rollback path if the atomic migration is incomplete or introduces an unanticipated break.
findings.location: S5 story description and AC bullet: '`acli*`/`adf`/`outbound_fields`/`comment_limits` reside under `adapters/jira/`… no re-export shims (S5)'
scenarios: A missed patch-string or unexpected import from outside the ~29 catalogued sites causes a test failure mid-S5; without a shim the only back-out is a full revert commit, which may conflict with subsequent work if S5 is split across PRs.
An integration consumer (CI script, import in a notebook, external tooling) imports the old path after S5 lands, silently breaking with no deprecation warning path.
findings.suggested_fix: Split S5 into S5a (add re-export shims at old paths + relocate files + migrate ~29 patch strings) and S5b (delete the shims after one full green CI cycle). This makes each sub-step independently revertable and gives an escape hatch if a missed import surface is discovered post-S5a.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add '800-LOC cap verified by pytest tests/unit/test_module_size_contract.py, exit 0' to S4's Acceptance Criteria.
evidence: Parent AC-5: 'Every src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/*.py stays within the 800-LOC module-size cap (.github/module-size-limit.txt) throughout.'
S4 AC has four bullet points; none references the 800-LOC cap or the module-size gate test.
S4 Testing section lists characterization, neutrality gate, unit, and teeth tests — no module-size contract test.
94f9 and dfb9 both explicitly include 'Every src/rebar/**/*.py stays within the 800-LOC cap — verified by pytest tests/unit/test_module_size_contract.py' in their AC; 6673 does not.
container pairing: parent + 6673-7636-a116-4f90/94f9-a9a0-7475-4e75/ce4d-c3ca-5412-4798/dfb9-d2d1-455d-48d9/ef33-eaf6-a545-4bfb
findings.finding: S4 (6673) does not include an explicit 800-LOC module-size gate in its Acceptance Criteria or Testing section, even though it is the largest change (routing 12 core sites and relocating Jira-specific logic) and is the story most likely to push a core module over the cap.
findings.location: child 6673-7636-a116-4f90 — Acceptance Criteria and Testing sections
scenarios: S4 relocates ADF-sizing, lossy status/parent mapping, duplicated value-maps, and link constants into adapters/jira/ — adapter modules could exceed 800 LOC; no gate catches this before the story is declared done.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to S4 AC: '[ ] Every src/rebar/**/*.py stays within the 800-LOC module-size cap after all 12 sites are rewired — verified by pytest tests/unit/test_module_size_contract.py, exit 0.'

findings.finding: S2 (ce4d) does not include an explicit 800-LOC module-size gate in its Acceptance Criteria or Testing section; the new _backend.py (5 role Protocols + 3 capability Protocols + RemoteRef + Backend facade) and adapters/jira/backend.py could breach the cap.
findings.location: child ce4d-c3ca-5412-4798 — Acceptance Criteria and Testing sections
scenarios: _backend.py containing 8 Protocol definitions plus RemoteRef and Backend facade could reach or exceed 800 LOC; no gate is specified for this story's landing.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to ce4d AC: '[ ] Every src/rebar/**/*.py stays within the 800-LOC cap after adding _backend.py and adapters/jira/backend.py — verified by pytest tests/unit/test_module_size_contract.py, exit 0.'

findings.finding: The S4 decomposition body counts '8 confirmed vendor-importing core modules AND 4 transport/construction sites' (implying 12 total sites to neutralize), but the S4 AC bullet says 'neutrality gate on the 10 differ/apply sites' — a numeric contradiction between sections that will send the implementer in two directions when writing the gate.
findings.location: Child decomposition S4 vs. Acceptance Criteria S4 bullet ('neutrality gate on the 10 differ/apply sites')
findings.suggested_fix: Add a parenthetical to the AC S4 bullet explaining the arithmetic: e.g., '…the 12 sites (8 vendor-importing modules + 4 transport/construction sites)' — or if 10 is correct, add '(8 core modules + 2 of the 4 construction sites; applier and fetcher are already indirect)' to the decomposition.

findings.finding: Parent AC3 states the neutrality gate covers '10 differ/apply sites,' but S4 confirms 12 rewired sites (8 vendor-importing core modules + 4 transport/construction sites). If the gate is implemented against the parent's stale count of 10, two sites risk being excluded from the gate, leaving them unchecked.
findings.location: child 6673-7636-a116-4f90 — Acceptance Criteria §1 (neutrality grep gate); parent AC3
findings.suggested_fix: Update parent AC3 to read 'neutrality gate on the 12 rewired sites' and list them explicitly; ensure S4's grep/test gate enumerates all 12 module names.
